goal: Choose an approach for adding real-time alerts on top of the existing batch analytics pipeline without disrupting current dashboard consumers
success_criterion: Decision documented with explicit trade-offs; implementable within one sprint by a single engineer; alert latency under target; no breaking changes for downstream consumers of the existing JSON data constants

candidates.summary: Polling-based alerts: extend the existing scheduled ingestion job to evaluate alert rules at the end of each cycle and emit notifications via the existing webhook channel. Reuses the YAML rule engine; no new infrastructure.
validation_criteria: Alert latency stays within 5 minutes of the ingestion cycle for all rule categories
Existing pipeline runtime grows by less than 10% over the current baseline
Alert rules are defined in the same YAML schema as classification rules (no new DSL)

candidates.summary: Event-driven alerts: introduce a Redis Streams message bus, refactor the pipeline to publish change events, and run an independent alert evaluator service that consumes the stream. Better long-term scalability but adds a new runtime dependency.
validation_criteria: End-to-end alert latency below 30 seconds at 10x current event volume
Pipeline throughput unchanged in a sustained load test (1 hour, 95th percentile within baseline)
Documented fail-over runbook for the Redis dependency with a tested recovery procedure

rationale: Candidate A scores high on Control (correctness, fit with the existing rule engine) and low on Conservator risk. Latency target is comfortably met by the polling cadence. Candidate B is more elegant and scales further, but Conservator flags implementation and operational overhead above the acceptable threshold for a single-engineer sprint; reversibility is also lower because of the new infrastructure dependency. Recommendation: ship A now, revisit B when alert volume exceeds ~10x the current baseline or when sub-minute latency becomes a real requirement.
